research.title: The Backend Is Now a Folder: Server-First Architecture in Next.js
research.whyTrending: With stable Server Actions and Functions, many teams ship apps where the entire backend lives inside the frontend repo — an architecture shift worth an opinionated take on when it works and when it bites.

seo.primaryKeyword: nextjs server actions vs separate backend
secondaryKeywords: when to use separate backend nextjs
seo.suggestedTitle: The Backend Is Now a Folder: When Server-First Bites

gaps: Most are pro/con listicles; few give a decision framework tied to concrete signals
The specific failure modes (queued sequential actions, hidden cross-feature coupling, testing the whole file tree) are scattered
No honest take on the migration cost when you outgrow it
strengths: tRPC comparison piece frames the architecture question well

officialSources.note: BFF pattern with server actions; cookie passthrough to REST microservice
server actions vs tRPC architecture tradeoffs

expertInsights: With stable server actions + functions the backend is often just a folder in the frontend repo
Server actions are queued -> sequential execution; bad for parallel data fetching
Overusing server actions -> hidden side effects and accidental cross-feature dependencies without boundaries
Overusing route handlers -> a mini-backend with leaky DTOs inside the frontend
Testing requires mocking the whole file structure; unclear which services auth depends on
Not ideal for websockets, background jobs, long-running processes, advanced auth
BFF variant: server actions call out to a REST microservice, passing cookies through
commonProblems: Sequential action waterfalls when actions used for data fetching
Cross-feature coupling as action files import each other
Hitting the wall on background jobs / websockets

strategy.angle: An opinionated architecture take: the backend-as-a-folder model is genuinely great for a large class of apps and quietly wrong for another — give the reader the specific SIGNALS that predict which side they are on (queued sequential actions, background jobs, multiple consumers, team boundaries) rather than a generic pros/cons list, and show the escape hatch (BFF passthrough) that lets you defer the decision
strategy.title: The Backend Is Now a Folder: When Server-First Next.js Bites
strategy.excerpt: Stable server actions turned the backend into a folder inside your frontend repo, and for a lot of apps that is exactly right. This is the honest other half: the concrete signals that tell you when server-first is quietly the wrong call, and the escape hatch that buys you time.
hooks: Contrarian: the backend as a folder is a feature and a trap, and the difference is predictable in advance
Gotcha: server actions are QUEUED, so using them to fetch data serializes your requests
Practical: a BFF passthrough lets you keep the DX now and split the backend later without a rewrite
outline: Hook: the folder that became a distributed system
How we got here: backend as a folder
Signal 1: you are fetching with actions (the queue trap)
Signal 2: background jobs, websockets, long-running work
Signal 3: more than one consumer of your data
